post: The 19th Amendment was a huge deal. And for reformers like Jane Addams and Florence Kelley, it was just the beginning. Addams founded Hull House in Chicago, working directly with the urban poor and immigrant communities. For her, women's political participation was never an end in itself, it was a means to improving everyday life through reforms to sanitation, housing, education, and the conditions of war. Kelley took that reformer spirit into public policy. Through her work with the National Consumers League, she fought for child labor laws, workplace safety standards, and limits on working hours, especially for women and children, so that children could be in school rather than on factory floors. The benefits, as the video notes, extended to everyone. Together, Addams and Kelley represent a vision of civic participation that goes beyond the ballot box. Voting wasn't the destination. It was the tool for economic opportunity, social reform, and a more just society. That vision is as relevant to civic education today as it was in 1920. Episode 8 of The Women's Declaration with Savannah Eccles Johnston, PhD (@savypolitics on IG) is live now 🎬 ▶️  https://bit.ly/4d3rMb6  #WomensHistoryMonth  #JaneAddams  #FlorenceKelley  #ProgressiveEra  #CivicsEducation  #AmericanHistory  #America250

Im excited to announce the June 2 publication of a new book Ive co-authored with my friend and colleague Tony Williams. Divided Over the Declaration: How an Enduring Debate Sustains the Vision of America will be out just in time for Americas 250th birthday. Every generation since the American founding has had to fight for the Declaration's promise. This book is the story of how the Declaration has been a source of divisionand unity. It features Frederick Douglass and abolitionism, Elizabeth Cady Stanton and womens suffrage, the 20th century civil rights movement, and what we must do today to uphold the principles of the Declaration. More to come on this in the months ahead. Barnes & Noble, Inc. members can pre-order with the code PREORDER25 at checkout for 25% off today through Thursday. Pre-orders at B&N matter more than most people realize because they help determine how books get placed and promoted in stores. If you're inclined, this is the window. Pre-order now at Barnes and Noble:  https://lnkd.in/eGgwJKPP  #DividedOverTheDeclaration  #B&N  #America250  #CivicsEducation  #AmericanHistory

The 2025 MyImpact Challenge Grand Prize winner was a high school student from Austin, Texas named William Mar. His project, Reach Through Rackets, provides accessible tennis programming for children with disabilities. He didn't start a corporation. He didn't lobby Congress. He picked up a racket, identified a gap in his community, and built something real to fill it. And he almost didn't submit. He looked at the other entries and convinced himself his project wasn't impressive enough. He won $10,000. This pattern shows up consistently in the MyImpact Challenge. The students most likely to hold back are often the ones doing the most meaningful work. They've built something real – quietly, persistently – and they just haven't been told it's worth recognizing. The $40,000 in prizes, including up to $500 for teachers, goes to students who showed up. Not the ones who assumed they didn't qualify. 👉  https://bit.ly/3fyGGr3  #MyImpactChallenge  #CivicsEducation  #StudentLeadership  #BillOfRightsInstitute

Every civics and history teacher knows the moment. You're mid-lesson. The content matters. And you can feel the room slipping away. It's not that students don't care about liberty, democracy, or the founders. It's that no one made it feel real to them yet. That's the problem BRI set out to solve, and games turned out to be one of the most powerful tools in the kit. BRI's interactive and tabletop games are already running in classrooms across the country, covering everything from American history timelines to federalism to the Bill of Rights. One of them, Regime, just won a gold medal at a global education gaming competition last year. All of it free. All of it scholar-reviewed. All of it designed to make students want to stay in the lesson. See the full library 👇  https://bit.ly/3PjADM8

Huge congratulations, Kimberly Huffman! 🏆 Thank YOU for your tireless work championing civic education and bringing the themes of the Declaration of Independence to life for your students. Were so proud to have you in the Civic Star community!

"Who's included?" That was the question Ida B. Wells forced the women's suffrage movement to answer over and over again. Wells was a journalist, activist, and suffragist from Chicago who worked to organize Black women politically at a time when the mainstream suffrage movement was content to leave them out. In 1913, she founded the Alpha Suffrage Club, one of the very first Black women's suffrage organizations in the country. That same year, she traveled to Washington, D. C. for the National Suffrage Parade. Organizers asked Black women to march at the back, behind the movement's pioneers, behind the working women's delegation, behind the state delegations, and even behind the male supporters of female suffrage. The stated reason: avoiding conflict with the southern delegation and keeping the movement from splitting. Wells refused. She and fellow Black suffragists stepped into their state delegations partway through the march, without incident. The message was precise and deliberate: "votes for women" had to mean all women. Not most women. Not White women first. ALL women. Its a message the movement had resisted since the 15th Amendment debate. Wells refused to let it be ignored. Episode 7 of The Women's Declaration with Savannah Eccles Johnston, PhD (@savypolitics on IG) is live now 🎬 ▶️  https://bit.ly/4rRtrV6  #WomensHistoryMonth  #IdaBWells  #WomensSuffrage  #BlackHistory  #CivicsEducation  #AmericanHistory  #America250

What if your love of civics and history took you somewhere unexpected this December? BRI's Teacher Fellowship is back, and this year, there's a track that could take one educator to the 2026 International Anti-Corruption Conference in the Dominican Republic. Fully funded. Fellows get behind-the-scenes access to BRI's staff and resources, a national network of like-minded civics and history educators, and a genuine hand in shaping the materials teachers across America use every day. The new Integrity and Transparency Fellow track is built for educators who want to go further, developing lessons on civic virtue, ethical leadership, and constitutional principles, and then bringing those lessons home from a global stage. Applications are open now. The deadline is April 30. If you've ever thought, "I want to do more than teach civics, I want to help build it," this is where that starts 👇  https://bit.ly/4cMEivv

Something historic is happening in classrooms across the country this year, and the Bill of Rights Institute wants to hear about it. The Civic Star Challenge is a weekly $300 prize drawing for teachers who share a Declaration of Independence or America's 250th Anniversary classroom moment. That's it. No essays. No applications. Just the real story of what's happening in your room during the most significant civics year in a generation. Every week, a new drawing. Every week, a chance to win. If you're a teacher, or if you know one, this is the moment to share it. Enter this week's drawing:  https://bit.ly/463KXxf  #CivicStarChallenge  #America250  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeacher  #BillOfRightsInstitute
